meta.title: SageReasoning Controlled Stoic Term Glossary
meta.purpose: Maps key Greek and Latin philosophical terms to their original meanings, standard transliterations, common English renderings, and notes on translation risk. Used as the authoritative reference when performing fresh translations from original texts to prevent inherited term-flattening from prior translators. Expanded in Phase 2 to cover complete Stoic corpus (Meditations, Discourses, Enchiridion, Letters, De Officiis, De Finibus, Diogenes Laertius Lives Book 7, Stobaeus Anthology). Expanded to v3.0.0 with 18 additional terms identified during complete Epistulae Morales translation.
meta.usage: When translating a passage, look up every technical term here first. Use the preferred_rendering unless context demands otherwise. Always flag when a standard English translation loses a distinction present in the original.

terms.literal_meaning: choosing-before; a prior commitment of the will
terms.translation_risk: HIGH — often flattened to 'choice' or 'will', losing the distinctively Stoic sense of an ongoing disposition of rational commitment that defines personal identity. Epictetus uses it as near-synonymous with selfhood.
terms.notes: Central to Epictetus. Not equivalent to modern 'free will' debate. Refers to the faculty of choice that is always 'up to us' (eph' hēmin).

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'mind' loses the Stoic specificity. The hēgemonikon is not just intellect but the seat of assent, impulse, and presentation.
terms.notes: The central processing faculty of the soul. Receives impressions (phantasiai), gives or withholds assent (synkatathesis), and generates impulse (hormē). Physical — located in the heart for orthodox Stoics.

terms.literal_meaning: an appearing; that which shows itself
terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'impression' can suggest passivity. Phantasia in Stoic usage covers the full range from sense-perception to propositional thought-content. Not equivalent to 'imagination'.
terms.notes: The starting point of all cognitive and practical life. The katalēptikē phantasia (comprehensive impression) is the Stoic criterion of truth.

terms.translation_risk: LOW — 'assent' is well-established and accurate. Risk is in failing to recognize its centrality: assent is where moral responsibility lives. Every passion, action, and belief requires assent.
terms.notes: The act of the hēgemonikon accepting an impression as true. Without assent, no impression can move to action. This is the locus of Stoic moral freedom.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'impulse' in English suggests something irrational. Stoic hormē is rational: it is an assented-to impression that something ought to be done. It is the direct cause of action.
terms.notes: Hormē and its opposite aphormē (aversion/impulse-away) are the two basic practical responses. Rational impulse (eulogos hormē) is what the sage has; excessive impulse is a passion (pathos).

terms.literal_meaning: that which comes down to one; what befits
terms.translation_risk: HIGH — 'duty' imports Kantian overtones completely foreign to Stoicism. Kathēkon refers to actions that can be given a reasonable justification in accord with nature, whether performed by sage or non-sage. It is NOT a moral absolute.
terms.notes: Distinguished from katorthōma (perfect/right action) which only the sage performs. Kathēkon includes things like caring for health, honoring parents — actions that admit of reasonable justification but don't require perfect virtue.

terms.translation_risk: HIGH — often conflated with kathēkon. The distinction is fundamental: katorthōma is kathēkon performed with perfect virtue (all four cardinal virtues present). Only the sage performs katorthōmata. This distinction drives the scoring system.
terms.notes: The gold standard of action in Stoic ethics. The scoring system measures proximity to katorthōma, not just whether an action is a kathēkon.

terms.literal_meaning: excellence; the quality of being best at one's function
terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'virtue' in modern English has moralistic/puritanical connotations. Stoic aretē is functional excellence: being excellent at being human. It encompasses practical skill, not just moral purity.
terms.notes: The four cardinal virtues (phronēsis, andreia, dikaiosynē, sōphrosynē) are species of aretē. Stoics held they are unified — possessing one fully entails possessing all.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'wisdom' alone is too vague (sophia is theoretical wisdom). 'Prudence' has become too narrow in English (implying caution). Phronēsis is the knowledge of what is truly good, bad, and indifferent applied to specific situations.
terms.notes: The master virtue for Stoics. Sub-virtues include: euboulia (good counsel), eulogistia (good reasoning), anchinoia (quick-wittedness), nounecheia (good sense).

terms.translation_risk: LOW for 'courage' — but note the gendered etymology. Stoic andreia is knowledge of what is truly fearful and what is not, and includes endurance (karteria) and magnanimity (megalopsychia).
terms.notes: Sub-virtues: karteria (endurance), tharraleos (confidence), megalopsychia (magnanimity), philoponia (industriousness).

terms.translation_risk: LOW for the word itself, HIGH for scope — Stoic justice is much broader than legal/distributive justice. It encompasses all social virtue: piety, good-heartedness, fair dealing, and the recognition of shared rational nature (oikeiōsis).
terms.notes: Sub-virtues: eusebeia (piety), chrēstotēs (good-heartedness), eukoinonēsia (good fellowship), eusynallaxia (fair dealing).

terms.translation_risk: HIGH — 'temperance' in English suggests mere moderation in drink. 'Self-control' is closer but still narrow. Stoic sōphrosynē is the knowledge of what is truly worth choosing and avoiding — it governs all desires and aversions, not just appetites.
terms.notes: Sub-virtues: eutaxia (good order), kosmiotēs (propriety), aidēmosyne (modesty), enkrateia (self-mastery).

terms.literal_meaning: having a good daimon; being well-spirited
terms.translation_risk: HIGH — 'happiness' is catastrophically misleading in modern English (implies subjective feeling-state). Stoic eudaimonia is an objective condition: living in accord with nature through the exercise of virtue. A person on the rack can be eudaimōn if virtuous.
terms.notes: This is the central concept of the flourishing score. Must be understood as an objective assessment of alignment with rational nature, not a feeling.

terms.translation_risk: CRITICAL — 'apathy' in English means not caring. Stoic apatheia means freedom from the four pathē (irrational emotions based on false judgements). The sage still has eupatheiai (good emotions): joy, rational wish, and caution.
terms.notes: The pathē are: hēdonē (pleasure from false good), epithumia (desire for false good), lupē (distress at false evil), phobos (fear of false evil). The eupatheiai are: chara (joy), boulēsis (rational wish), eulabeia (rational caution).

terms.preferred_rendering: natural affiliation / self-ownership extending to others
terms.translation_risk: HIGH — no single English word captures it. Describes the natural process by which a being first recognizes itself and its constitution, then extends concern outward to family, community, and all rational beings. Foundation of Stoic social ethics.
terms.notes: Two aspects: personal oikeiōsis (self-preservation/self-recognition) and social oikeiōsis (extending circles of concern). Hierocles' concentric circles model derives from this.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'indifferent' in English suggests 'doesn't matter at all.' Stoic adiaphora include things with selective value (axia) like health, wealth, reputation — they're indifferent only to virtue, not to life. The proēgmenon/apoproēgmenon distinction is critical.
terms.notes: Three categories: proēgmena (preferred), apoproēgmena (dispreferred), and strict adiaphora (neither). This maps directly to indifferents.json.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — the standard rendering 'preferred indifferent' is accurate but can confuse newcomers. The key point: these have selective value (axia) and are rationally chosen when no virtue conflict exists, but they are NEVER goods.
terms.notes: Examples: health, wealth, reputation, good birth, physical ability. Opposite: apoproēgmenon (dispreferred).

terms.literal_meaning: word; account; reason; ratio; proportion; argument
terms.translation_risk: CRITICAL — logos is the most polysemous term in Greek philosophy. In Stoic usage it can mean: (1) the cosmic rational principle pervading nature, (2) individual human reason, (3) a verbal account/argument, (4) a ratio or proportion. ALWAYS specify which sense in translation.
terms.notes: The formula 'living in accordance with logos/nature' (homologoumenōs tē physei zēn) is the Stoic summum bonum. Context determines whether cosmic logos or human rational faculty is meant.

terms.literal_meaning: upon us; up to us; in our power
terms.preferred_rendering: up to us / within our control
terms.translation_risk: LOW — but the phrase is so central that its scope must be precisely understood. What is eph' hēmin: assent, impulse, desire, aversion, and the use of impressions. What is NOT: body, property, reputation, office.
terms.notes: The foundational distinction of Epictetan ethics. Maps directly to the dichotomy of control in the scoring system.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — important Stoic physical concept. The soul's tonos determines its strength: a soul with strong tonos has firm assent and is not swayed. Weak tonos = vulnerability to passions. Often omitted in translation.
terms.notes: Explains the physical basis of moral strength in Stoic psychology. The pneuma (breath/spirit) has two motions: inward (creating cohesion/identity) and outward (creating quality/perception).

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'progress' is fine but the concept itself is complex. The prokoptōn (one making progress) is still, technically, a fool (not a sage) — but progressing. Stoics debated whether there are degrees within prokopē. Critical for the scoring system.
terms.notes: Directly relevant to the flourishing score: users are prokoptontes (those making progress), not sages. The system measures prokopē, not arrival at sagehood.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — modern 'sympathy' is an emotion. Stoic sympatheia is a physical-cosmological principle: all parts of the cosmos affect each other because the cosmos is a single living organism permeated by pneuma.
terms.notes: Grounds the Stoic cosmopolitan ethics: if the cosmos is one organism, all rational beings share in the same logos and are fellow-citizens.

terms.literal_meaning: a seizing; grasp; laying hold of
terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — often oversimplified to 'grasp.' Katalēpsis is specifically the secure mental possession of a truth through the katalēptikē phantasia. It is the cognitive state that justifies assent and belief.
terms.notes: The sage achieves katalēpsis reliably; the fool is subject to doksa (mere opinion). Katalēpsis is what motivates the sage's unwavering assent.

terms.literal_meaning: a grasping/seizing impression; a comprehensive impression
terms.translation_risk: CRITICAL — the Stoic criterion of truth. A katalēptikē phantasia is one that bears the marks of truth with such force that it naturally compels assent. It is not merely subjectively compelling but carries objective truth-marks.
terms.notes: Distinguished from false impressions that merely seem graspable. The katalēptikē phantasia is stamped with such clarity that it cannot be confused with a false impression — unless the subject is diseased or deceived.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — the term is less commonly discussed than hormē but equally important. Aphormē is the rational counterpart to hormē: if hormē is 'impulse toward', aphormē is 'impulse away.' Both are rational in the sage; excessive aphormē manifests as phobos (fear).
terms.notes: Complements hormē in the Stoic action theory. A virtuous agent engages both rational impulses (toward good) and rational aversions (from harm), always proportionate to true value.

terms.translation_risk: HIGH — orexis is rational desire (characteristic of the sage), distinct from epithumia (irrational craving). This distinction is fundamental: orexis aligns with logos and virtue; epithumia does not.
terms.notes: Orexis for genuine goods (virtue, wisdom) is virtuous. Irrational craving (epithumia) for false goods (pleasure, wealth) is a passion. Confusing them is a cardinal translation error.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — the rational counterpart to phobos (irrational fear). Ekklisis is the sage's measured withdrawal from genuine evils (disunity with virtue); phobos is irrational shrinking from false evils.
terms.notes: Works in tandem with orexis: rational reaching toward good paired with rational aversion from evil. Both are virtues when properly calibrated.

terms.translation_risk: CRITICAL — the context determines everything. When hēdonē is discussed as a pathos (passion), it is an irrational emotion based on false judgement: elation at a supposed present good. NOT the benign satisfaction of living well.
terms.notes: One of four pathē (irrational emotions). Hēdonē from false goods (bodily sensation, luxury) clouds judgment. The sage experiences no hēdonē; instead, chara (rational joy) at genuine goods.

terms.translation_risk: CRITICAL — epithumia is NEVER virtuous in Stoic philosophy. It is irrational reaching for a supposed future good (pleasure, wealth, glory). Distinguished rigorously from orexis (rational desire) and boulēsis (rational wishing).
terms.notes: One of four pathē. The starting point for many vices: the craving for false goods leads to fear (of losing them), distress (if lost), and envy. Eradicated in the sage through correct judgment.

terms.translation_risk: HIGH — lupē as a pathos is not ordinary pain or sadness, but irrational emotional contraction at the (false) belief that a present evil obtains. Distinct from the sage's acknowledgment of bodily discomfort.
terms.notes: One of four pathē. Results from judgment that something evil (disunity with virtue) is present. Absent in the sage, who recognizes that only vice is truly evil.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — phobos is irrational anticipatory contraction based on the false belief that a future evil threatens. Distinguished from eulabeia (rational caution about genuine evils).
terms.notes: One of four pathē. The Stoic cure: recognize that the only genuine evil is lack of virtue; death, poverty, and disease cannot harm the virtuous person. Rationalize and the fear dissolves.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — chara is a eupatheias (good emotion), not a pathos. It is rational elation at a genuine good, especially the presence of virtue and wisdom. It has solidity that irrational hēdonē lacks.
terms.notes: The sage experiences chara when making moral progress, when recognizing the cosmic order, when living in accordance with nature. It is neither restless nor fleeting.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — boulēsis is a eupatheias (good emotion): rational reaching toward a genuine good, especially wisdom and virtue. It reflects deliberate commitment, not blind appetite.
terms.notes: Rational counterpart to the passion epithumia (craving). The sage's boulēsis fixes on what is truly good; the fool's epithumia chases false goods. This distinction is central to therapy.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — eulabeia is a eupatheias (good emotion): rational aversion from genuine evils (especially actions contrary to virtue). Distinguished from phobos (irrational fear).
terms.notes: Completes the triad of good emotions with chara and boulēsis. The sage practices eulabeia toward vice and only vice; no rational caution toward poverty, disease, or death.

terms.literal_meaning: the beautiful; the fine; the noble
terms.preferred_rendering: the fine / the noble / moral beauty
terms.translation_risk: HIGH — 'the good' is too vague. To kalon is specifically the moral beauty of virtue, the only thing truly praiseworthy. It is what naturally attracts the rational will.
terms.notes: Epictetus and Marcus Aurelius invoke to kalon as the object of rational desire. Recognizing the kalon (beauty) of virtue is key to overcoming passions born of false values.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — axia attaches to preferred indifferents (health, wealth, reputation). It is NOT virtue (aretē), which alone is absolute good. Indifferents have axia when reason approves their pursuit absent virtue-conflict.
terms.notes: Maps to the scoring system's concept of 'selective value': indifferents are chosen based on their axia (worth in the context of living reasonably).

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — telos is often 'end' or 'goal' but context is critical. The Stoic telos is eudaimonia: living in accord with nature through virtue. All virtuous action aims at this telos.
terms.notes: The telos gives structure to the scoring system: actions are evaluated by their proximity to the ultimate end (eudaimonia through virtue).

terms.translation_risk: HIGH — the central summum bonum formula. Often misread as 'follow your instincts' or 'return to nature.' In fact, it means living in conscious accord with universal rational nature (logos), not mere animal nature.
terms.notes: The formula that resolves apparent Stoic paradoxes. Living 'in agreement with nature' = exercising reason in alignment with cosmic reason = virtue. This is eudaimonia.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — autarkeia is a cardinal virtue: the virtuous person is self-sufficient for eudaimonia. Not that they need nothing (they have a body, live in a polis), but that virtue cannot be taken or destroyed by external force.
terms.notes: The foundation of Stoic equanimity. If virtue alone makes you eudaimōn, and virtue is up to you, then you are self-sufficient — dependent on no one, undefeatable by circumstance.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — pneuma is the physical substrate of all Stoic causation: soul is pneuma, logos is pneuma-organized, even virtue is a state of pneuma-tension. English 'spirit' is too immaterial; it's physical but not material in our sense.
terms.notes: Two motions of pneuma: inward (creating cohesion/identity) and outward (creating sensation and action). The tonos (tension) of pneuma correlates with moral strength.

terms.literal_meaning: burning up; conflagration; eruption into fire
terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — the periodic dissolution of the cosmos into primordial fire, followed by recreation. Part of the Stoic eternal recurrence doctrine. Easy to confuse with mere destruction; it's cyclical renewal.
terms.notes: Supports Stoic fatalism and cosmic order: the universe unfolds according to eternal law, cycles through ekpyrōsis, and recreates identically. This grounds universal providential ordering.

terms.literal_meaning: that which is bound/fastened; destiny; fate
terms.translation_risk: CRITICAL — heimarmenē is often misread as 'necessity that overrides choice.' Actually: the unbreakable causal chain determined by logos. The sage assents to heimarmenē willingly — reconciling freedom (in assent) with necessity (in outcomes).
terms.notes: Central to Stoic ethics: 'Want what will happen anyway.' Not resignation but rational alignment with inevitable causal order. The sage's freedom is freedom to assent, not to alter fate.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — pronoia is the rational, providential ordering of the cosmos by logos/god. Not a personal being with emotions but rational necessity that serves all parts of the whole. Supports theodicy against objections.
terms.notes: Stoic response to suffering: what appears evil to the part serves the good of the whole. Providence is rational, not sentimental. This grounds the sage's willing acceptance (amor fati).

terms.translation_risk: LOW — but the concept is often sentimentalized. The Stoic cosmopolitan recognizes kinship with all rational beings due to shared logos, not mere sentiment. Duty to all humans flows from this metaphysical fact.
terms.notes: Supports universal oikeiōsis: if cosmos is one organism and all humans share logos, then all humans are family. This grounds duties to strangers, enemies, slaves, and enemies of state.

terms.literal_meaning: love of humanity; kindness to humans
terms.preferred_rendering: love of humanity / benevolence toward humans
terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — philanthropia is not sentiment but a virtue: the rational recognition of shared human nature and the resulting duty to aid and not harm. Flows from the recognition of oikeiōsis.
terms.notes: Manifests in concrete duties: honoring parents, protecting the vulnerable, contributing to common goods. It is a form of justice (dikaiosynē) applied specifically to humanity.

terms.literal_meaning: [Latin] that which is fitting/suitable; [Greek] what is fitting/appropriate
terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — decorum is Cicero and Seneca's Latin rendering of Greek to prepon (the fitting/appropriate). In context, it means: what befits a rational agent given their nature, role, and circumstances.
terms.notes: Similar to kathēkon but often with more emphasis on social role and propriety. The wise person perceives and performs actions that fit their circumstances.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — constantia in Seneca denotes unwavering commitment to virtue, a steadiness of judgment that doesn't waver with circumstance. Core to the sage's character.
terms.notes: Related to tonos (tension of pneuma): constantia is the psychological manifestation of strong pneuma. It is what prevents the fool's oscillation between passions.

terms.literal_meaning: [Latin] tranquility of mind; [Greek] non-disturbance; freedom from perturbation
terms.preferred_rendering: tranquility of mind / freedom from perturbation
terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — not passive numbness but active equanimity grounded in correct judgment. Seneca uses tranquillitas animi; Marcus uses ataraxia. Both indicate the peaceful state of the sage whose judgments are stable.
terms.notes: Result of apatheia (freedom from destructive passions) combined with correct understanding of value. The sage's tranquility is robust, not fragile; it withstands circumstance.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — the proficiens (prokoptōn) is not yet sage but demonstrably progressing in virtue. Still technically a fool (all non-sages are fools) but on the path. Critical category for grounding human effort.
terms.notes: Users of the scoring system are profilens (making progress). The system measures prokopē (progress), not arrival. This category explains why sustained effort matters.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'leisure' suggests passivity. Stoic otium is purposeful withdrawal for philosophical study, not idleness. Seneca devotes multiple letters to justifying it.
terms.notes: Contrasts with negotium (business/public life). For Seneca, otium is justified only when used for philosophy and writing that benefits posterity. Not mere relaxation.

terms.translation_risk: LOW — but its pairing with otium as a philosophical contrast is often lost in translation.
terms.notes: Etymologically the negation of otium. The otium-negotium tension is central to Seneca's practical philosophy.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — the Latin carries a sense of being 'seized' by external affairs that mere 'occupation' misses. Seneca uses it pejoratively.
terms.notes: Occupati (the preoccupied) are contrasted with those who have genuine otium. The word implies involuntary capture by trivial affairs.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — modern 'security' implies protection from external threats. Latin securitas is an internal psychological state: the absence of worry (cura).
terms.notes: Related to but distinct from tranquillitas animi. Securitas emphasises the negative (absence of anxiety), tranquillitas the positive (settled calm).

terms.preferred_rendering: meditation on death / rehearsal for death
terms.translation_risk: LOW — but must not be confused with morbid fixation. Stoic meditatio mortis is a practical exercise for freedom from fear.
terms.notes: Part of the broader practice of premeditatio malorum (premeditation of adversity). Aims at freedom, not depression.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'malorum' are not true evils in Stoic doctrine but indifferents; the exercise involves rehearsing the loss of preferred indifferents.
terms.notes: A core Stoic spiritual exercise. By mentally rehearsing hardship, one reduces its power to disturb when it arrives.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'foolishness' sounds trivial. Stoic stultitia is the default condition of all non-sages: a fundamental moral-cognitive disorder, not mere silliness.
terms.notes: The stultus (fool) is technically every human who has not achieved sagehood. Stultitia is the opposite of sapientia (wisdom). ALWAYS render as 'folly', never 'foolishness'.

terms.literal_meaning: wisdom, the state of the sage
terms.translation_risk: LOW — but note that Stoic sapientia is not mere intellectual knowledge. It is a complete transformation of character involving all four cardinal virtues.
terms.notes: The sapiens (sage) possesses sapientia. In Stoic doctrine, sapientia and virtus are coextensive.

terms.translation_risk: HIGH — 'wise man' is both gendered and too casual. The Stoic sapiens is a near-impossible ideal: a person who has achieved complete virtue and rationality. ALWAYS use 'the sage', never 'wise man'.
terms.notes: The sage is the Stoic ideal figure against whom all action is measured. Whether any sage has ever existed was debated within the school.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — often conflated with heimarmene (fate). Necessitas in Seneca can refer to both cosmic necessity and immediate compulsion (e.g. death as necessitas).
terms.notes: In Stoic physics, necessitas is the unbreakable chain of causation. In Seneca's ethics, it often appears as the ultimate argument for accepting death calmly.

terms.translation_risk: HIGH — translating as 'emotion' imports modern psychology. Latin perturbatio (Cicero's rendering of Greek pathos) refers specifically to irrational movements of the soul that the sage eliminates. NOT all emotions.
terms.notes: Cicero's standard rendering of Stoic pathos. The four genera: aegritudo (distress), metus (fear), libido (craving), laetitia (irrational elation). Distinct from eupatheiai (rational emotions the sage does experience).

terms.literal_meaning: a having, holding; a stable condition
terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'state' is too vague. Hexis in Stoic physics is a specific grade of pneumatic tension that holds material objects together and, in psychology, refers to a settled character trait.
terms.notes: Part of the Stoic hierarchy: hexis (cohesive state) → physis (nature/growth) → psyche (soul) → logike psyche (rational soul). Virtues and vices are both hexeis.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — 'exercise' sounds physical. Stoic exercitatio encompasses both physical ascetic practices (cold baths, fasting) and mental disciplines (premeditatio, meditatio mortis).
terms.notes: Related to Greek askesis. The term covers the full range of Stoic practical disciplines aimed at moral progress.

terms.translation_risk: LOW — but note that Stoic usage is always pejorative: indulgentia toward oneself is a failure of self-discipline (sophrosyne).
terms.notes: Contrasts with Stoic temperance. Seneca warns repeatedly against indulgentia corporis (bodily indulgence).

terms.literal_meaning: fortune, chance, what is brought by circumstances
terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — must not be confused with heimarmene (fate/deterministic causation). Fortuna in Seneca is the personified face of external contingency—what happens to you, not the cosmic causal order.
terms.notes: Seneca frequently personifies fortuna as an adversary or trainer. Fortuna controls externals; virtue is immune to it. ALWAYS render as 'fortune', not 'luck' or 'chance'.

terms.translation_risk: HIGH — 'happiness' imports hedonistic and modern subjective-wellbeing connotations entirely foreign to Stoic thought. ALWAYS render beatus/beata vita as 'flourishing' / 'the flourishing life'.
terms.notes: Latin equivalent of Greek eudaimonia. The title 'De Vita Beata' = 'On the Flourishing Life' in SR rendering. Beatus used of persons = 'flourishing', not 'happy'.

terms.translation_risk: HIGH — easily confused with beatitudo/eudaimonia. Felicitas in Seneca usually refers to external worldly success, NOT the Stoic flourishing life. Translating as 'happiness' collapses a crucial distinction.
terms.notes: Distinguish carefully from beatitudo. Felicitas is an external condition (preferred indifferent at best); beatitudo/eudaimonia is the internal state of virtue. Render felicitas as 'prosperity' or 'worldly success', NEVER as 'happiness' or 'flourishing'.

terms.translation_risk: MEDIUM — in Stoic psychology, symphonia refers specifically to the internal harmony of judgments and impulses within the ruling faculty, not merely musical or social harmony.
terms.notes: Related to homologia (consistency/agreement with nature). The sage's mind exhibits symphonia — all judgments, desires, and impulses are mutually consistent.
